section.title: Great Are the Works of the Lord
section.sectionRationale: An acrostic hymn celebrating God's mighty works, this psalm forms a matched pair with Psalm 112, the twin describing the flourishing life of the one who fears the LORD in response to the great God this psalm describes.
section.thesis: God's works, from creation to covenant to redemption, are great, trustworthy, and worth wholehearted praise, and the fear of this gracious, covenant-keeping LORD is the very beginning of wisdom.
section.frameNote: Three movements following the Hebrew acrostic's flow: praise for the greatness and enduring righteousness of God's works, verses 1-4; His covenant provision and power shown in giving the nations' inheritance, verses 5-6; and the trustworthy precepts and redemption that ground the fear of the LORD as wisdom's beginning, verses 7-10.

units.label: Great are the works of the LORD

verses.text: Hallelujah! I will give thanks to the LORD with all my heart in the council of the upright and in the assembly.
Great are the works of the LORD; they are pondered by all who delight in them.
Splendid and majestic is His work; His righteousness endures forever.
He has caused His wonders to be remembered; the LORD is gracious and {{rachum|compassionate}}.
He provides food for those who fear Him; He remembers His {{berit|covenant}} forever.
He has shown His people the power of His works by giving them the inheritance of the nations.

units.label: The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom

verses.text: The works of His hands are truth and justice; all His precepts are trustworthy.
They are upheld forever and ever, enacted in truth and uprightness.
He has sent redemption to His people; He has ordained His covenant forever; holy and awesome is His name.
The fear of the LORD is the beginning of {{chokmah|wisdom}}; all who follow His precepts gain rich understanding. His praise endures forever!

rachum.gloss: Compassionate, tender in mercy, from the same root as womb, picturing a parent's deep tenderness. Paired with gracious, chanun, this phrase echoes God's self-revelation to Moses in Exodus 34:6.

berit.gloss: Covenant, a solemn binding agreement. God's remembrance of His covenant forever is the ground of both His provision for His people, verse 5, and the redemption He has sent them, verse 9.

chokmah.gloss: Wisdom, skillful living rightly ordered toward God. The fear of the LORD, reverent awe before His holiness, is named as its very starting point, a truth repeated across the wisdom literature, Proverbs 1:7, 9:10.

basic.context: This acrostic hymn, each of its twenty-two lines beginning with a successive letter of the Hebrew alphabet, praises God's great works from creation through covenant to redemption, forming a twin with Psalm 112, which describes the flourishing life that results from fearing this God.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm moves from admiration of God's works to confidence in His covenant provision to the conclusion that reverent fear of Him is where true wisdom begins, teaching that right knowledge of God overflows into right living.
basic.originalLanguages: God is rachum, compassionate, and chanun, gracious, verse 4, echoing His self-revelation to Moses in Exodus 34:6. His berit, covenant, anchors both His provision, verse 5, and His redemption, verse 9. And chokmah, wisdom, is said to begin precisely where reverent fear of Him begins, verse 10.
basic.typology: The redemption God has sent His people, verse 9, and His covenant ordained forever anticipate the greater and final redemption accomplished in Christ, who secures an everlasting covenant sealed in His own blood, Luke 22:20, Hebrews 13:20.
basic.christ: The redemption this psalm celebrates as already sent, verse 9, finds its fullest and final form in Christ, whose blood secures an everlasting covenant, Hebrews 13:20, and in whom the wonders of God, verse 4, are supremely revealed, since He is Himself the Word through whom all God's works were made, John 1:3.
basic.god: God is shown as great in works, gracious and compassionate in character, faithful to remember His covenant across generations, and trustworthy in every precept He gives, holy and awesome in name yet near enough to provide food for those who fear Him.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 34:6 supplies the language of gracious and compassionate that this psalm echoes. Luke 1:49-55, Mary's Magnificat, mirrors this psalm's themes of God's mighty deeds and covenant remembrance. Hebrews 13:20 names the everlasting covenant sealed in the blood of Jesus, the ultimate redemption this psalm anticipates.
basic.oneStory: The great works of God, from creation to the giving of the land to sending redemption, all flow from His covenant faithfulness and compassionate character, a pattern completed in Christ, whose redemption and everlasting covenant fulfill this psalm's praise and whose person is the very wisdom that reverent fear of the LORD leads toward.

context.literary: An alphabetic acrostic, each half-verse beginning with a successive Hebrew letter, twenty-two segments in all, a structure signaling completeness, that God's praiseworthy works span from A to Z, and forming a matched pair with the similarly structured Psalm 112.
context.historical: Likely sung in post-exilic temple worship when renewed emphasis on covenant, Torah, and communal praise was needed, its opening Hallelujah and communal assembly setting fitting the corporate thanksgiving of restored worshipers.
context.canonical: Twinned with Psalm 112, this psalm describes the great God while its companion describes the flourishing life of the one who fears Him, together forming a single lesson in piety and its fruit.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To lead worshipers from admiring meditation on God's great works into confident trust in His covenant faithfulness, concluding that reverent fear of this God is the necessary starting point for all true wisdom.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes God's historical works, creation, covenant, conquest, and redemption; it prescribes the ongoing pattern of meditating on these works, delighting in them, and living in reverent fear that issues in following His trustworthy precepts.

debates.question: Does verse 6, giving them the inheritance of the nations, refer only to the conquest of Canaan or does it anticipate a wider inheritance?

views.label: The verse refers specifically to Israel's historical conquest and possession of Canaan
views.case: The immediate context of covenant, provision, and God's works among His people fits the concrete historical inheritance of the promised land, as in Joshua 21:43-45.

views.label: The verse anticipates a wider, typological inheritance fulfilled in the church's inheritance in Christ
views.case: The New Testament repeatedly reframes the promised inheritance in terms of the believer's inheritance in Christ, Ephesians 1:11, suggesting the historical conquest foreshadows a greater fulfillment.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm the same truth: God powerfully and faithfully grants His people an inheritance they did not earn, whether the land of Canaan historically or the fuller inheritance secured in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: God is rachum, compassionate, and gracious, verse 4, echoing His self-revelation in Exodus 34:6. His berit, covenant, is the ground of both provision, verse 5, and redemption, verse 9. And chokmah, wisdom, is declared to begin precisely at reverent fear of Him, verse 10, a truth repeated throughout the wisdom literature.

typology.type: The redemption sent to His people, verse 9
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ's redemption accomplished at the cross, sealing an everlasting covenant in His own blood, Hebrews 13:20.

typology.type: The fear of the LORD as the beginning of wisdom, verse 10
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge, Colossians 2:3, the wisdom this psalm points every reverent worshiper toward.

study.christ: The redemption this psalm celebrates as already accomplished, verse 9, finds its fullest and final form in Jesus Christ, whose blood establishes the everlasting covenant, Hebrews 13:20. Christ is Himself the wisdom in whom this psalm's promised understanding is fully realized, Colossians 2:3, and the wonders of God's work, verse 4, are supremely displayed in Him, through whom all things were made, John 1:3.
study.god: God is great in His works, gracious and compassionate in character, faithful to remember His covenant across every generation, and trustworthy down to every precept He gives, holy and awesome, yet near enough to provide daily food for those who fear Him.

crossReferences.note: Supplies the language of gracious and compassionate that this psalm directly echoes in describing God's character.
Mary's Magnificat mirrors this psalm's themes of God's mighty deeds, covenant remembrance, and holy name.
Names the everlasting covenant sealed in Christ's blood, the ultimate redemption this psalm anticipates in verse 9.

study.oneStory: The great works of God, spanning creation, covenant, provision, and redemption, all flow from His gracious and compassionate character, a pattern brought to completion in Christ, whose redemption seals an everlasting covenant and who is Himself the wisdom that reverent fear of the LORD leads every worshiper to find.

sermon.title: God at Work
sermon.why: Keller preaches Psalm 111 within the series Open My Lips: Studies in the Psalms, teaching on a powerful, involved God, a supernaturally changed life, and how to connect the power of God to one's own life, drawing on the twin relationship between Psalm 111 and Psalm 112.

quiz.q: Where does the psalmist resolve to give thanks to the LORD, according to verse 1?

options.text: With all his heart, in the council of the upright and in the assembly.
options.note: The praise is both wholehearted and public, private devotion overflowing into corporate worship.

options.text: Only in private, away from any public gathering.
options.note: The verse explicitly names the council of the upright and the assembly, a public setting.

options.text: Only when circumstances are comfortable and easy.
options.note: No such condition is placed on the thanksgiving described; it is a resolved, wholehearted commitment.

options.text: Through written psalms alone, never spoken aloud.
options.note: The setting described, the assembly, implies spoken, communal praise, not merely written composition.

quiz.q: What does verse 5 say God provides, and what does He remember forever?

options.text: He provides food for those who fear Him and remembers His covenant forever.
options.note: This pairs daily provision with covenant faithfulness as the ground of security.

options.text: He provides wealth for the powerful and forgets the poor.
options.note: The verse specifically names those who fear Him, not the powerful, as recipients of provision.

options.text: He provides only military victories, with no mention of covenant.
options.note: The verse explicitly names both food and the remembrance of His covenant, not merely military matters.

options.text: He provides temporary blessings that expire after one generation.
options.note: The text says He remembers His covenant forever, not for a single generation only.

quiz.q: According to verse 10, what is the beginning of wisdom?

options.text: The fear of the LORD.
options.note: This foundational wisdom principle is echoed throughout Proverbs, 1:7 and 9:10.

options.text: Accumulating great wealth.
options.note: The verse identifies reverent fear of the LORD, not wealth, as wisdom's foundation.

options.text: Following the customs of surrounding nations.
options.note: The verse points to following God's own precepts, not foreign customs.

options.text: Avoiding all forms of worship and religious practice.
options.note: The psalm as a whole is a call to worship, the opposite of avoiding religious practice.

quiz.q: How does verse 9 describe what God has done and ordained for His people?

options.text: He has sent redemption to His people and ordained His covenant forever.
options.note: This redemption anticipates the fuller redemption accomplished in Christ, sealed in an everlasting covenant.

options.text: He has withdrawn all covenant promises from His people.
options.note: The verse says the opposite; He has ordained His covenant forever, not withdrawn it.

options.text: He has sent redemption only to a select few individuals.
options.note: The verse describes redemption sent to His people broadly, not a narrow select group.

options.text: He has ordained a covenant that expires at the end of each century.
options.note: The text explicitly says forever, not a covenant with a fixed expiration.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's picture of redemption and covenant connect to Christ?

options.text: Christ's blood seals the everlasting covenant, fulfilling the redemption this psalm celebrates as already sent.
options.note: Hebrews 13:20 names this everlasting covenant sealed in Jesus' blood, completing what this psalm anticipates.

options.text: The psalm has no meaningful connection to the New Testament at all.
options.note: The psalm's language of redemption and everlasting covenant is directly picked up in Hebrews 13:20.

options.text: It connects only to the Old Testament conquest of Canaan, with no further significance.
options.note: While the historical conquest is one referent, the psalm's covenant and redemption language reaches forward typologically to Christ.

options.text: It predicts the exact date of a future political kingdom in Jerusalem.
options.note: The psalm speaks of covenant faithfulness and redemption in general terms, not a specific future political date.

questions: The psalmist resolves to give thanks with all his heart, both privately and in the assembly. Is your own praise of God more private than public, or more public than heartfelt? What would it look like to bring both together this week?
God is described as gracious and compassionate, echoing His self-revelation to Moses. Where do you need to remember God's tender compassion rather than imagining Him as distant or harsh?

The psalm says God remembers His covenant forever and provides for those who fear Him. What specific provision in your life right now can you trace back to God's covenant faithfulness rather than mere circumstance?

Wisdom is said to begin with the fear of the LORD, not with cleverness or human achievement. In what area of decision-making this week do you need to start with reverent fear of God rather than your own reasoning alone?

This psalm's redemption and covenant are fulfilled in Christ's blood, which seals an everlasting covenant. How does knowing your covenant with God can never expire change the way you face present uncertainty?

translationNotes.note: Hallelujah transliterates the Hebrew Hallelu Yah, meaning Praise the LORD, and functions as both an opening exclamation and a summons to the assembly.
Gracious and compassionate translates chanun and rachum, the same pairing of terms God uses to describe Himself to Moses in Exodus 34:6.

The psalm is an acrostic; in Hebrew each of its twenty-two half-verses begins with a successive letter of the alphabet, a structural device signaling the comprehensive, A to Z completeness of God's praiseworthy character and works.

Verified 10 verses in order against the section map bounds Psalm 111:1-10. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming Tim Keller's sermon 'God at Work' at gospelinlife.com, preached at Redeemer Presbyterian Church on August 25, 2013, Scripture Psalm 111.
